What I'm Listening To

The podcasts that keep me company in the workshop — usually while sanding through four grits or waiting for glue to cure. Good for the hands-busy, mind-free hours. Ordered by personal favorites first, then alphabetical.

Letters from an American
Heather Cox Richardson
History professor Heather Cox Richardson puts today's news in historical context. Essential, grounding, and quietly reassuring that we've been here before.
99% Invisible
Radiotopia / PRX
Roman Mars on the unnoticed design and architecture that shapes the world. Every episode makes you see something ordinary in a completely new way.
Radiolab
WNYC Studios
Science, philosophy, and human experience told through layered, immersive sound. One of the best-produced shows in podcasting.
This American Life
PRX / WBEZ Chicago
The original long-form radio storytelling show. Theme-driven episodes, unforgettable characters. Still essential after 30 years.
The Anthropocene Reviewed
Complexly
John Green reviews facets of the human-centered planet on a five-star scale. Tender, funny, and unexpectedly moving.
Benjamen Walker's Theory of Everything
Radiotopia / PRX
Strange, digressive, and deeply intelligent. Walker follows ideas wherever they lead — often somewhere you didn't expect.
Cautionary Tales with Tim Harford
Pushkin Industries
Tim Harford excavates historical disasters to find the lessons we keep failing to learn. Rigorous and compulsively listenable.
Criminal
Radiotopia / PRX
Stories about people who've done wrong, been wronged, or gotten caught somewhere in between. Phoebe Judge's voice alone is worth the subscription.
The Daily
The New York Times
Twenty minutes of news every morning from the NYT. The gold standard for daily news podcasts — reported, not just read.
Ear Hustle
Radiotopia / PRX
Stories of life inside and outside prison, made by incarcerated and formerly incarcerated people. Honest, human, and unlike anything else in podcasting.
The Economics of Everyday Things
Freakonomics Radio Network
Short episodes on the hidden economics behind ordinary objects and systems. Why does a banana cost what it costs? Now you'll know.
Everything is Alive
Radiotopia
Unscripted interviews with inanimate objects — a can of cola, a grain of sand, a bar of soap. Stranger and more profound than it sounds.
Freakonomics Radio
Freakonomics Radio Network
Stephen Dubner applies economic thinking to things economists don't usually touch. Wide-ranging, counterintuitive, reliably interesting.
Fresh Air
NPR / WHYY
Terry Gross in conversation with writers, musicians, actors, and thinkers. Forty-plus years of the best interviews in public radio.
Heavyweight
Gimlet / Spotify
Jonathan Goldstein helps people go back and deal with things left unresolved. Funny and devastating in equal measure.
Hyperfixed
Radiotopia / PRX
Alex Goldman (co-creator of Reply All) investigates dilemmas submitted by listeners — from minor annoyances to life decisions. Rigorous, warm, and genuinely useful.
The Infinite Monkey Cage
BBC Radio 4
Brian Cox and Robin Ince explore science with comedians and scientists. Proof that rigor and levity aren't mutually exclusive.
The Journal
WSJ / Spotify
Wall Street Journal reporters on the biggest stories in business and tech. Concise, well-sourced, and a good counterweight to longer-form listening.
Love and Radio
Luminary
Intimate audio portraits of people on the fringes — told with great care and unusual restraint. Nothing else sounds quite like it.
The Memory Palace
Radiotopia / PRX
Nate DiMeo tells small, forgotten stories from history with extraordinary care. Some of the finest writing in podcasting, period.
The Moth
The Moth
True personal stories told live, without notes. Funny, devastating, surprising — sometimes all three in ten minutes.
On the Media
WNYC Studios
Brooke Gladstone and Bob Garfield examine how the press covers the news — and how the news shapes the culture. Essential media literacy.
Planet Money
NPR
Economics made genuinely entertaining. They find the weird, human story inside every financial system. Consistently surprising.
Radio Diaries
Radiotopia / PRX
Ordinary people document their own extraordinary lives. One of the oldest forms of narrative radio, still done better here than anywhere.
Revisionist History
Pushkin Industries
Malcolm Gladwell reexamines overlooked or misunderstood events in the past. Not always right, but always worth arguing with.
Science Friday
PRX
Ira Flatow on science news, research, and discovery. Friday afternoons in the workshop just got smarter.
Science Vs
Spotify
Wendy Zukerman puts popular health and science claims against the evidence. Smart, funny, and genuinely useful.
Search Engine
PJ Vogt
PJ Vogt investigates questions about how the modern world actually works. The kind of reporting that makes you feel smarter and more confused at the same time.
Serial
Serial Productions / NYT
The podcast that proved the format could sustain a long story. Season one remains a benchmark for narrative nonfiction audio.
The Skeptics Guide to the Universe
SGU Productions
Steven Novella and panel on science, critical thinking, and pseudoscience. A weekly inoculation against bad reasoning.
SmartLess
WonderyMedia / Amazon Music
Jason Bateman, Sean Hayes, and Will Arnett interview guests who don't know who they're talking to until they show up. Genuinely fun.
Tangle
Tangle
Isaac Saul summarizes the best left and right arguments on a political issue, then gives his own take. Rare good-faith journalism in a polarized world.
Up First from NPR
NPR
Three big stories, ten minutes, every morning. The fastest way to know what's happening before you pick up a tool.
Wait Wait... Don't Tell Me!
NPR / WBEZ
NPR's weekly news quiz with Peter Sagal. The best argument that news and comedy belong together.
What It's Like To Be... with Dan Heath
Pushkin Industries
Dan Heath asks people with unusual jobs or experiences to describe what it's actually like. Quietly fascinating.
What's Your Problem?
Pushkin Industries
Jacob Goldstein talks to entrepreneurs and inventors about the hard problems they're trying to solve. Optimistic without being naive.

36 shows · updated as the playlist grows

The Newsletter

When a tool is done, I write it up. No schedule—just the story.
